page_subtitle: Professor-style OLS forecasting model using raw non-engineered gold factors.

model_status.high_yield_rule: high_yield excluded from the main regression because it starts too late for the core multivariate model window.

model_explanation: This page uses ordinary least squares regression as a classical forecasting method.
The first model uses all raw non-engineered factors available in the core multivariate dataset.
gold_lag_1, gold_ma_20, trend, and high_yield are not allowed in the regression.
The second model removes non-significant predictors based on training-period p-values.
The final regression is rerun using significant raw factors only.
Validation performance is estimated using only the training period for fitting.
Test performance is estimated after fitting on training plus validation.

candidate_metric_table.formula: gold_price ~ real_yield + nominal_yield + tips_curve + fed_bs + usd_index + vix_index + fin_stress + gpr_index + policy_unc + gld_tonnes + oil_wti + unrate + inflation + m2_supply + eur_usd + jpy_usd + ppi_index + ind_prod + cap_util
gold_price ~ real_yield + nominal_yield + fed_bs + usd_index + fin_stress + gpr_index + gld_tonnes + oil_wti + unrate + inflation + eur_usd + jpy_usd + ppi_index + ind_prod + cap_util

limitations: OLS regression can be sensitive to multicollinearity among macroeconomic predictors.
Removing gold_lag_1 and gold_ma_20 makes the model more factor-driven but may reduce forecast accuracy.
A statistically significant coefficient does not prove causation.
Residual autocorrelation may remain because gold prices are time-series data.
This is one forecasting candidate and should be compared against ARIMA, SARIMAX, XGBoost, and other models later.
